coredata.dc:title: Multi-functionalized hyaluronic acid nanogels crosslinked with carbon dots as dual receptor-mediated targeting tumor theranostics
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Xu
Han, Yu
Pei, Mingliang
Zhao, Xubo
Tian, Kun
Zhou, Tingting
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Hyaluronic acid (HA)-based theranostic nanogels were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy, by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid (FA-PEG-HA) with carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Due to the extraordinary fluorescence property of the integrated CDs, the theranostic nanogels could be used for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells. HA could load Doxorubicin (DOX) via electrostatic interaction with a drug-loading capacity (DLC) of 32.5%. The nanogels possessed an ideal release of DOX in the weak acid environment, while it was restrained in the neutral media, demonstrating the pH-responsive controlled release behavior. The cytotoxicity and cellular uptake results clearly illustrated that most DOX was released and accumulated in the cell nuclei and killed the cancer cells efficaciously, due to their dual receptor-mediated targeting characteristics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor theranostics
Hyaluronic acid
Carbon dots
Dual receptor-mediated targeting
Tumor microenvironment-responsive controlled release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid (FA-PEG-HA)
Folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid crosslinked with carbon dots (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) nanogels
Preparation and characterization of the (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) nanogels

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was granted financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 20904017 ) and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University of the Ministry of Education of China (Grant No. NCET-09-0441 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the proposed DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs theranostic nanogels for the intracellular release.
Outline for the synthesis of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels.
TEM images and hydrodynamic diameters of the CDs (a, c) and FA-PEG-HA-CDs (b, d).

PL emission spectra of CDs (a) (0.1mg/mL) and FA-PEG-HA-CDs (b) (2.5mg/mL) aqueous dispersions and normalized PL emission spectra of CDs (c) and FA-PEG-HA-CDs (d) aqueous solutions under different wavelength excitations, and PL intensity Insets: Fluorescence images of CDs and FA-PEG-HA-CDs aqueous dispersions under UV light.

pH-responsive release of DOX from DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs at 37°C (a) and the cell viability of SKOV3 cells by the MTT assay, after incubation with the FA-PEG-HA-CDs and DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs (corresponding concentration of DOX) and free DOX for 24h (b), data are presented as the mean±standard deviation (SD; n=6), the asterisks (*) represented P<0.001 between the two groups, respectively, when the P value was less than 0.05, differences were considered statistically significant.

Cellular uptake of SKOV3 cells stained by Hoechst and incubation with the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs (20μg/mL) after 12h by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) technique. From left to right, cell nuclei images are stained by Hoechst (a), DOX fluorescence in cells (b) and merged images (c).
DOX release data fitted in models of Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas.

head.ce:title: Multi-functionalized hyaluronic acid nanogels crosslinked with carbon dots as dual receptor-mediated targeting tumor theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hyaluronic acid (HA)-based theranostic nanogels were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy, by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid (FA-PEG-HA) with carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Due to the extraordinary fluorescence property of the integrated CDs, the theranostic nanogels could be used for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells. HA could load Doxorubicin (DOX) via electrostatic interaction with a drug-loading capacity (DLC) of 32.5%. The nanogels possessed an ideal release of DOX in the weak acid environment, while it was restrained in the neutral media, demonstrating the pH-responsive controlled release behavior. The cytotoxicity and cellular uptake results clearly illustrated that most DOX was released and accumulated in the cell nuclei and killed the cancer cells efficaciously, due to their dual receptor-mediated targeting characteristics.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy remains one of the cornerstones in cancer treatment. However, the cytotoxic drugs cause severe side effects in the therapeutic process, due to their non-specific delivery. To reduce the side effects and improve the antitumor activity of such drugs, the nanoscaled smart drug delivery systems (DDS) with desired stimuli-responsive controlled release performance have been intensively studied in the recent years (Chacko, Ventura, Zhuang, & Thayumanavan, 2012). They could accumulate in the tumor sites through the passive targeting function via enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect due to their small size (Maeda, Nakamura, & Fang, 2013), as well as the active targeting effect by modification with targeting ligands, such as folate, which could recognize the folate receptor, a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-linked membrane glycoprotein, overexpressed in tumor tissues (Low, Henne, & Doorneweerd, 2008).

Most recently, integrating the diagnosis and chemotherapy functions into a theranostic platform makes it easily to real-time and noninvasively monitor the therapeutic effects during the chemotherapy treatment course (Pei et al., 2015), by introducing the fluorescent organic molecules (Jia et al., 2016) or inorganic nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2010), luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals (Ho & Leong, 2010), MRI contrast agents (Guo et al., 2015), and so on.

The excellent biodegradability and biocompatibility of the polysaccharides, as well as plentiful chemical functionalities, make them most promising carriers for DDS (Goodarzi, Varshochian, Kamalini, Atyabi, & Dinarvand, 2013). Especially for hyaluronic acid (HA), it could target the tumor tissues as an ideal biologically active substance for drug delivery, owing to the specific recognization by the glycoprotein CD44 overexpressed in tumor tissues (Tripodo et al., 2015).

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of biologically compatible carbon nanomaterial, has been recognized as an emerging class of fluorescent materials and provide a broad application potential in biomedicine (Hola et al., 2014), due to its downconversion photoluminescent (PL) properties, inherently low cytotoxicity, and amphiphilic characteristics with abundant functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl. They have been recently used as a bioimaging substance in tumor theranostics. For example, Zheng et al. designed a theranostic nanomedicine by means of the condensation reaction between the amino groups on the surface of fluorescent CDs and the carboxyl group of the oxaliplatin derivative Oxa(IV)-COOH, integrating the optical properties of CDs and the anticancer function of oxaliplatin for the simultaneous drug delivery and fluorescent tracking (Zheng et al., 2014).

In the present work, extraordinary hyaluronic acid (HA)-based theranostic nanogels (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) were prepared by crosslinking the folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified HA (FA-PEG-HA) with CDs. This system was designed for the PL imaging and dual receptor-mediated targeting controlled delivering DOX (Scheme 1 ).

Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Low molecular weight sodium hyaluronate (MW =400,000, cosmetic grade) was provided by Shandong Freda Biopharm. Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (99%) was purchased from Rionlon Bohua (Tianjin) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co. Ltd. Poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (H2N-PEG-NH2, Mn=4000, 99%) was got from Beijing Kaizheng Biotech Development Co. Ltd. Folic acid (FA, 97%) was got from Tianjin Guangfu Technology and Development Co., Ltd. Dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCC, 95%) was bought from Shanghai KEFENG Chemical Reagent Co., Inc. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl, 99%) was provided by was provided by J&K Chemical co., LtD. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4%) was provided from Beijing Huafeng United Technology Co. Ltd. Double-deionized water was used throughout.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis as reported previously (Cheng et al., 2014). Citric acid anhydrous (2.0g, 10.41mmol) was dissolved in 20mL water in a 100mL beaker. Then, ethylenediamine (0.936g, 15.57mmol) was added dropwise into the abovementioned solution with vigorous stirring, obtaining a clear, transparent solution. The beaker was placed in a domestic microwave oven (700W) and sustained for 4min. After cooling down to room temperature, the product was dissolved in water, and dialyzed (MWCO of 1000) for 3days to remove the impurity. Finally, the CDs were collected by lyophilization.

Folic acid (0.2207g, 0.5mmol) was dissolved in 10mL of anhydrous DMSO, and then triethylamine (0.11mL, 0.79mmol) was added with stirring. Furthermore, DCC (0.1032g, 0.5mmol) and NHS (0.0575g, 0.5mmol) were added into the abovementioned solution. The reaction mixture was stirred at ambient temperature in the dark for 8h and filtered off the generated precipitate (Dicyclohexylurea, DCU). 10mL DMSO solution of H2N-PEG-NH2 (2g, 0.5mmol) was added into the filtrate. The reaction was conducted for 48h at room temperature. Then, the mixture was disposed by dialysis (MWCO of 1000) with water for 3days to remove DMSO. Finally, the product of FA-PEG-NH2 was collected by lyophilization (Singh, Gupta, Asthana, & Jain, 2008). The substituting degree of product was calculated as 85.08 mol% by measuring the absorption with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid (FA-PEG-HA)
ce:section.ce:para: 1g of sodium hyaluronate was dissolved in 100mL of water. Then, EDC·HCl (0.4753g, 2.48mmol) and NHS (0.2854g, 2.48mmol) were added into the solution and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 4.5 with HCl aqueous solution. The activation process was lasted for 4h at room temperature. And then, FA-PEG-NH2 (1.1011g, 0.25mmol) was added into the mixture solution with vigorous stirring, and the mixture was stirred for 48h at room temperature. Finally, it was dialyzed (MWCO of 14,000) with water for 3days to remove the impurities and the resultant product (FA-PEG-HA) was collected by lyophilization (Zhao et al., 2014). It was found that average 5.1 FA-PEG molecules were grafted onto per 100 repeating units of HA, by measuring the absorption with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid crosslinked with carbon dots (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5g of FA-PEG-HA was dissolved in 100mL of water. Then, EDC·HCl (0.2377g, 1.24mmol) and NHS (0.1427g, 1.24mmol) were added into the FA-PEG-HA solution. After the pH value was adjusted to 4.5, activation process was lasted for 4h at room temperature. Afterwards, 0.025g of CDs was added as crosslinking agent into the aforementioned solution, and the reaction mixture was stirred for 48h. The resulting solution was disposed by dialysis (MWCO of 14,000) for 3days and purified with a PD10 column. Finally, the FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels were collected by lyophilization (Goh et al., 2012).
The morphology and diameter of the CDs and FA-PEG-HA-CDs were characterized with a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM).
1H NMR spectra were characterized with a Nuclear Magnetic Resonance instrument (JEOL ECS 400M) at 400MHz with the solvent of deuterium oxide.

The number average molecular weight (Mn) of H2N-PEG-NH2 and FA-PEG-NH2 (5mg/mL) were measured by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) with tetrahydrofuran as the eluent at the flow rate of 1.0mL/min at 35°C, after calibration with polystyrene standard.

The number average molecular weight (Mn) the FA-PEG-HA (5mg/mL) was measured by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) with the solution of 0.2mol/L NaNO3 with 0. 02% NaN3 as the eluent at the flow rate of 1.0mL/min at 35°C, after calibration with dextran (273kDa) standard (Synytsya, Kim, Kim, & Park, 2010).
The steady-state emission spectra of the CDs and FA-PEG-HA-CDs were detected in their aqueous dispersions with a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer.
The hydrodynamic diameter and size distribution of the products were measured with dynamic scattered light (DLS, BI-200SM) in their aqueous dispersions at ambient temperature.
The DOX-loading capacity and controlled release of the nanogels was measured with a UV–vis spectrometer (TU-1901) at indoor temperature.

DOX was selected as a model drug to evaluate the drug-loading and controlled release performance of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels. Briefly, 10mg of the dry FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels were added into 2mL of 2mg/mL DOX·HCl solution, and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 7.4. And then, the solution was diluted to 4mL with pH 7.4 PBS. The solution was stirred in the dark for 48h at room temperature. The DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels were collected by centrifugation, and the precipitate was washed with pH 7.4 PBS to remove the unloaded DOX. The drug-loading capacity (DLC) and drug-loading efficiency (DLE) of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels were counted by the formula below, calculated with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 480nm (Lin et al., 2014).
(1)
DLC
( % )
=
initial mass of DOX − mass of DOX in supernatant
mass of nano gels
× 100 %
(2)
DLE
( % )
=
mass of DOX in nanogels initial mass of DOX
× 100 %

Then the in vitro release of DOX from the drug loaded system was studied at 37°C in three different buffer solutions individually: pH 5.0 acetate buffer; pH 6.5 acetate buffer, and pH 7.4 phosphate buffer. Each sample was dispersed in 10mL of above-mentioned buffer solution respectively and transferred into a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut off of 14,000. And then, the dialysis bag was immersed into 120mL corresponding buffer and mildly shaken at 37°C. At presupposed time, 5mL of the solution was pipetted from the solution, and the mass of the released DOX was measured with UV−vis at 480nm. To keep the solution volume constant, an equal volume of corresponding fresh buffer solution was replenished after each sampling.

The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide) assay was used to evaluate the cytocompatibility of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels. As to the MTT assay, the cells were implanted into the 96-well plate with the density of 1×105 cells per well. Then, a series of concentrations of the prodrug or corresponding polymer precursor were added into the cells respectively, and incubated for 24h at 37°C. Then, 20μL of MTT solution (5mg/mL) was added into each well, and incubated for another 4h. The solution was removed carefully, and then, 150μL of DMSO was added per well. The plates were shaken for 20min. The absorbance of each well was measured by the Enzyme-linked Immunosorbent Assay Appliance at the wavelength of 490nm.

The cellular uptake was exhibited by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) using human ovarian cancer cell SKOV3 line after 12h incubation. The cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33258 and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. So the site of intracellular fluorescence was confirmed by using CLSM with excitation wavelengths of 480nm for DOX and 405nm for Hoechst 33258.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: The folate-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) modified hyaluronic acid crosslinked with carbon dots (FA-PEG-HA-CDs) nanogels were fabricated as Scheme 2 . The amino-riched fluorescent CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine (Cheng et al., 2014). The morphology of the CDs was near spherical with the mean diameter of 2.5nm (Fig. 1 a). Their hydrodynamic diameter was also ascertained by using dynamic light scattering (DLS) of about 4.3nm in hygrometric state due to swelling (Fig. 1c). From Fig. 2 a, it can be seen that the CDs showed a strong PL emission peak centered at 447nm and exhibits strong blue PL emission which can be easily seen with the naked eye when it was excited at a wavelength of 365nm (insert). Furthermore, the normalized fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2c) demonstrated that the emission peak had a red shift with increasing the excitation wavelength from 370 to 430nm, namely the wavelength excitation-dependent behavior (Zhang et al., 2012).

The FA-PEG-NH2 was synthesized via the DCC-activated amidation of one of the terminated amino group of NH2-PEG-NH2 with FA in DMSO (Singh et al., 2008). It could be confirmed by the GPC analysis that the number average molecular weight (Mn) increased from 4190 to 4998 after modification with FA. Then the FA-PEG-NH2 was grafted onto the HA backbone by an EDC-activated amidation between the amino group of FA-PEG-NH2 and carboxylic acid group of HA (Zhao et al., 2014). The chemical shift of the methene (CH2) protons in PEG at δ =3.64ppm appeared in the 1H NMR spectrum of the product FA-PEG-HA (Fig. S1), confirming that the FA-PEG-NH2 had been successfully conjugated onto HA (Zhang, Xia, Wang, & Li, 2009) with a number average molecular weight (Mn) increased to 391,970.

After the crosslinking of the FA-PEG-HA with the amino-riched CDs, the spherical shaped FA-PEG-HA-CDs nanogels were obtained with particle size and hydrodynamic diameter of about 20nm (Fig. 1b) and 43nm (Fig. 1d), respectively. The chemical shifts of PEG and CDs at δ =3.64ppm and δ =1.13 in the 1H NMR spectrum of the product revealed that the FA-PEG-HA had been crosslinked with the amino-riched CDs as crosslinker (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the fluorescence character of the CDs was maintained even after it was modified FA-PEG-HA, also with the tunable fluorescence intensity with different excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b and d), only with a slight red shift of the maximum emission length to 451nm due to the amidation as reported previously (Jin, Kim, Jun, Hong, & Jeon, 2013).

DOX, a wide-spectrum anticancer drug which could effectively inhibit the proliferation of the SKOV3 cells (Hu, Wu, Du, You, & Yuan, 2008), was used as a model drug to evaluate the drug-loading and controlled release performance of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs theranostic nanogels. The DLC and DLE of DOX were calculated by UV–vis analysis to be 32.5% and 81.25%, respectively. The carboxyl groups in HA are deprotonated and the amino group of DOX is protonated in pH 7.4 solution due to their pKa of 3.0 (Huh, Zhao, & Kim, 2015) and 8.3 (Sanson et al., 2010), so DOX was easily loaded onto the FA-PEG-HA-CDs via the electrostatic interaction, with ideal loading capacity.

Compared to the normal tissue under physiological conditions (pH 7.2–7.4), the tumor microenvironment was faintly acidic (pH 6.5–7.2) because of the increased fermentative metabolism and poor perfusion. Other than the external pH of tumor microenvironment, there exists a pH gradient from early endosome (pH 6–6.5), late endosome (pH 5–6) to lysosomes (pH 4.5–5) (Estrella et al., 2013; Kaur, Prasad, Balakrishnan, & Banerjee, 2015). So the in vitro controlled release of DOX from the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs was investigated in three buffer solutions respectively (pH 5.0 acetate buffer (mimicking the endosome and lysosomes), pH 6.5 acetate buffer (mimicking the endosome) and pH 7.4 phosphate buffer (mimicking the normal physiological media)) (Fig. 3 a). The release of DOX was very ascendant in pH 5.0 solution with a the cumulative release ratio of 44.7% within 8h, while it was very low in pH 7.4 solution with only 11.4%. As time goes on, the release of DOX increased slowly. The cumulative release ratio reached 55.5% and 27.9% at pH 5.0 and pH 6.5 within 38h, respectively. Distinctly, only 14.7% of DOX was released in pH 7.4 buffer saline. The DOX cumulative release increased with the increase of media acidity, showing pH responsive controlled releasing characteristic. It is due to the interaction between DOX and the FA-PEG-HA-CDs was reduced with the concentration increase of hydrogen ion, as well as the enhancement of DOX solubility in water (Deng et al., 2016).

The DOX release model was fitted with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas equation (Fig. S2) with the fitted parameters summarized in Table 1 . All the values of R2 were greater than 0.92, demonstrating that the linear coefficients were ideal. According to the Higuchi theory (Kalia & Guy, 2001), the DOX release behavior was diffusion-controlled at pH 5.0 because the value of k was greater than 1, while it was nondiffusion controlled release in the other two media. For the Korsmeyer–Peppas models, the release in all the three buffer solutions was nonFickian diffusion, because the values of n were all greater than 0.5 (Costa & Sousa Lobo, 2001).

The cytocompatibility of the FA-PEG-HA-CDs and DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs was evaluated in SKOV3 cells by using MTT assays with the concentrations of 5, 10, 20, and 40μg/mL (Fig. 3b). It was easily to discover that the FA-PEG-HA-CDs had an ideal cytocompatibility within the concentration range of 5–40μg/mL and the distribution of the cell survival ratio was in 104–96.7% within 24h. On the contrary, it decreased sharply, only 53.5% at the concentration of 40μg/mL, after the addition of the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs into the SKOV3 culture solution. The killing efficiency of the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs was only a little lower than the free DOX in SKOV3 cells, although only a half DOX had been released from the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs within 24h. The results declared that the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs had an efficient inhibition ratio of the cancer cells propagation.

The cellular uptake experiment of the DOX-loaded FA-PEG-HA-CDs was tested by CLSM for SKOV3 cells after 24h incubation (Fig. 4 ). The typical DOX fluorescence appeared in the image under the excitation wavelengths of 480nm (Fig. 4b), indicating that DOX was released very availably from the prodrug at the weakly acidic media of tumor tissues. In the merged images (Fig. 4c), the location of DOX was superposed with Hoechst 33258. This phenomenon manifested that the most of the released DOX was located in the cell nuclei, thus it could effectively embed into the DNA and RNA and inhibit the nucleic acid duplication (Jia et al., 2015).

In summary, hyaluronic acid-based theranostic nanogels, functionalized with folic acid and PEG, and crosslinked with the biocompatible and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), were designed for the tumor diagnosis and chemotherapy for the first time, showing perfect biocompatibility, dual receptor-mediated targeting function, ideal fluorescence property, and desirable tumor microenvironment-responsive controlled release performance. It would provide a promising approach for the optical imaging-directed chemotherapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was granted financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 20904017) and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University of the Ministry of Education of China (Grant No. NCET-09-0441).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2016.06.109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer nanogels: a versatile nanoscopic drug delivery platform
Gold nanocages: a novel class of multifunctional nanomaterials for theranostic applications
Polycation-b-polyzwitterion copolymer grafted luminescent carbon dots as a multifunctional platform for serum-resistant gene delivery and bioimaging

Acidity generated by the tumor microenvironment drives local invasion

Biomineralized biomimetic organic/inorganic hybrid hydrogels based on hyaluronic acid and poloxamer
Fluorescent copolymer-based prodrug for pH-triggered intracellular release of DOX
Novel fluorescent pH/reduction dual stimuli-responsive polymeric nanoparticles for intracellular triggered anticancer drug release
Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups
Trigger responsive polymeric nanocarriers for cancer therapy
Amphiphilic miktoarm star copolymer (PCL)3-(PDEAEMA-b-PPEGMA)3 as pH-sensitive micelles in the delivery of anticancer drug
Discovery and development of folic-acid-based receptor targeting for imaging and therapy of cancer and inflammatory diseases

A simple method to achieve high doxorubicin loading in biodegradable polymersomes
Folate and folate-PEG-PAMAM dendrimers: synthesis, characterization: and targeted anticancer drug delivery potential in tumor bearing mice
Structure and antitumour activity of fucoidan isolated from sporophyll of Korean brown seaweed Undaria pinnatifida

European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics
High intensity focused ultrasound-responsive release behavior of PLA-b-PEG copolymer micelles
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Biocompatible graphene oxide nanoparticle-based drug delivery platform for tumor microenvironment-responsive triggered release of doxorubicin
Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine
